title: Jenkins & Selenium Integration
overview: How to integrate Selenium automated tests into a Jenkins CI/CD pipeline for continuous testing.
whyItMatters: Running Selenium tests locally doesn't scale. Integrating them into Jenkins ensures tests run automatically on every code commit, providing immediate feedback on build health.
explanation: Jenkins can checkout code from Git, build it using Maven, and execute Selenium tests. You configure a Jenkins job (Freestyle or Pipeline) to run `mvn test`. Jenkins can also archive test reports (like TestNG or Surefire XML), publish HTML reports, and send email notifications based on the build outcome. Running tests on a headless browser or Selenium Grid is common in Jenkins environments.
keyPoints: Jenkins runs automated tests on triggers (e.g., code push, schedule).
Uses Maven or Gradle to execute tests via command line (`mvn test`).
Test reports are parsed using Jenkins plugins (JUnit, TestNG Results).
Pipeline as Code (Jenkinsfile) is the modern way to define CI/CD jobs.
realWorldExample: When a developer merges a PR, Jenkins triggers a build, deploys to a staging server, and runs the Selenium smoke test suite. If tests pass, the team is notified via Slack; if they fail, the build is marked unstable.
commonMistakes: Running UI tests on a Jenkins server without a display (needs Headless mode or Xvfb).
Failing to clean up browser processes, leading to memory exhaustion on the Jenkins node.
Hardcoding environment URLs in tests instead of passing them via Jenkins parameters.
bestPractices: Run tests in headless mode (e.g., ChromeOptions.addArguments("--headless")) when executing on Jenkins.
Use Jenkins parameters to pass environment variables (e.g., browser type, target URL) to Maven.
Archive ExtentReports or Allure reports as Jenkins artifacts for easy debugging.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you run Selenium tests in Jenkins if the Jenkins server has no GUI?
interviewQuestions.a: By configuring the WebDriver to run in Headless mode (e.g., Chrome Headless), or by configuring Xvfb (X virtual framebuffer) on a Linux server to simulate a display.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you pass a browser parameter from Jenkins to your Selenium framework?
interviewQuestions.a: Define a string parameter in the Jenkins job (e.g., BROWSER). In the build step, use `mvn test -Dbrowser=$BROWSER`. In the Java code, retrieve it using `System.getProperty("browser")`.

revisionSummary: Integrate via Jenkinsfile -> Git checkout -> Maven test -> JUnit/TestNG report parsing -> Notifications. Run headless and parameterize variables.
